message.content: Adversaries may exploit the Proc filesystem on Linux to perform OS Credential Dumping, targeting credentials stored in memory. The Proc filesystem contains extensive information about the running operating system, and processes with root privileges can access and scrape live memory of other programs. If these programs store passwords or password hashes in memory, adversaries can harvest these credentials for immediate use or brute-force attacks. Tools like MimiPenguin and LaZagne facilitate this by dumping process memory and extracting clear-text credentials or hashes using text strings and regex patterns. Common targets include applications like Gnome Keyring, sshd, and Apache. Mitigation strategies include enforcing strict access controls, using memory-safe programming practices, and employing security tools to monitor and restrict access to the Proc filesystem. Regularly updating and patching systems can also reduce vulnerabilities that adversaries might exploit.
